cur-nrsa-individual-predoctoral-fellowsh.why: This fellowship supports predoctoral trainees still working toward their PhD, but Jordan finished his PhD in 2019 and is now a senior postdoctoral researcher years past that stage. cur-arpa-h-biomedical-data-fabric-bdf-to.why: This program already made its ~20 awards in 2024 and isn't currently accepting new applicants, so there's nothing for Jordan to apply to right now. Even setting timing aside, it's a general-purpose data-interoperability infrastructure effort rather than a mechanism aimed at lymphoma biology or his specific multi-omics research questions.

cur-damon-runyon-dale-f-frey-award-for-b.why: The Dale F. Frey Award is not an open competition — it's reserved for scientists who are already current or recent Damon Runyon or Quantitative Biology Fellows. Nothing in Jordan's profile indicates he holds one of those fellowships, so he isn't in the eligible pool for this transition-to-independence award.

cur-postdoctoral-diversity-enrichment-pr.why: PDEP's $60K enrichment award is specifically for postdocs from underrepresented groups, and nothing in Jordan's profile establishes that he qualifies under that criterion, so eligibility can't be assumed here. If that criterion doesn't apply to him, the fit is weak regardless of how well his computational oncology background otherwise matches the general biomedical scope.

cur-nih-director-s-transformative-resear.why: This award funds exceptionally unconventional, paradigm-shifting projects and does allow team-based applications, which could theoretically touch Jordan's multi-omics integration work. However, it is generally applied for by an independent PI leading the effort, and Jordan's mentored postdoc status makes him a weaker fit as lead applicant compared to a faculty-level investigator.

cur-nigms-maximizing-investigators-resea.why: MIRA (R35) is structured around a single flexible grant for an investigator's own NIGMS-mission research program, which requires PI status Jordan doesn't yet hold as a mentored postdoc. It's also housed at NIGMS rather than NCI, so even the general biomedical framing doesn't compensate for the missing cancer-specific and independent-investigator fit.

cur-nlm-career-development-award-in-biom.why: This K01 is a mentored career-development award meant to carry an informatics/data-science postdoc like Jordan toward independence, which aligns well with his computational and machine-learning expertise and his current mentored senior-postdoc stage. It isn't cancer-specific the way his lymphoma work is, so it's a solid mechanism fit rather than a topical bullseye, but it's one of the few awards here actually built for someone at his career stage.

jhu-postdoctoral-fellowship-lymphoma-res.why: This is a direct match: the Lymphoma Research Foundation's fellowship funds exactly the disease Jordan studies, T-cell lymphoma, and is structured as an early-career postdoctoral award rather than requiring an independent lab. His computational oncology and single-cell/multi-omics skill set is a natural fit for the kind of mechanistic and translational lymphoma questions this fellowship supports.

jhu-proposals-in-leukemia-research-when-.why: This RFP stays within Jordan's broader blood-cancer/hematology wheelhouse and is open to investigators at various career stages, which works in his favor. But it's specifically an acute-leukemia award rather than lymphoma, so the disease-specific alignment with his T-cell lymphoma research is only partial, and the 2026 cycle has already closed with no 2027 date posted yet.

jhu-clra-research-grants-children-s-leuk.why: CLRA funds leukemia research with a pediatric leukemia orientation, which sits outside Jordan's core focus on T-cell lymphoma and adult computational oncology. The shared blood-cancer category isn't enough to overcome the disease-specific mismatch, and this cycle's deadline has already passed with the next one not yet posted.

cur-postdoctoral-enrichment-program-pdep.why: Jordan's status as a senior postdoc in computational oncology fits the career stage this Burroughs Wellcome award targets, but the PDEP is specifically built to enrich training for underrepresented postdoctoral researchers, and nothing in his profile confirms that eligibility criterion. If he does qualify, the modest $20,000/year could support travel, coursework, or supplies, but it's a career-development supplement rather than a research grant for his T-cell lymphoma or multi-omics work.

cur-pew-scholars-program-in-the-biomedic.why: Pew Biomedical Scholars is explicitly reserved for newly independent assistant professors, and Jordan is a senior postdoc without his own lab, so he isn't yet eligible to apply. His computational oncology and multi-omics research would suit the program's appetite for creative biomedical science once he holds a faculty appointment, but that's a future consideration, not a current one.

cur-mathematical-and-physical-sciences-a.why: This NSF fellowship sits in the mathematical and physical sciences directorate and is aimed at broadening participation in those fields, which doesn't align with the cancer genomics and single-cell immunology work Jordan actually does. The solicitation is also archived with an uncertain future, adding a practical barrier on top of the topical mismatch.

cur-ai2050-early-career-fellowship-schmi.why: Jordan's machine-learning toolkit overlaps loosely with what Schmidt Sciences funds, but AI2050 centers on foundational AI problems rather than applied cancer genomics or T-cell lymphoma biology. It's also a closed, nomination-only process with no open application, so there's no direct path for him to pursue it.

cur-sloan-research-fellowship-alfred-p-s.why: Sloan Research Fellowships go to untenured faculty running independent labs, and Jordan is a mentored senior postdoc without his own position, so he falls outside the basic eligibility bar. His genomics and computational expertise could be competitive here later, but not at his current career stage.

cur-arpa-h-sbir-sttr-fy2026-topics-advan.why: This is an SBIR/STTR mechanism built for small businesses commercializing technology, not for an academic postdoc embedded in a mentored lab, so Jordan couldn't apply as an individual researcher. The FY2026 Solution Summary deadline has also already passed, closing this particular window.

cur-nih-director-s-early-independence-aw.why: The Early Independence Award is designed for outstanding new PhDs to skip the postdoc entirely and launch an independent lab immediately, which doesn't match Jordan's situation roughly seven years past his PhD and well into a mentored postdoc. His research is strong, but this mechanism targets a different point in the career pipeline than where he is now.

jhu-cri-irvington-postdoctoral-fellowshi.why: Jordan's work on T-cell lymphoma, immunotherapy, and computational cancer immunology sits squarely in the hypothesis-driven immunology research this flagship CRI fellowship supports, and the program is built for exactly his career stage as a mentored postdoctoral researcher. The three-year stipend would give him room to push his single-cell and multi-omics approaches to immuno-oncology further before moving toward independence.

jhu-aacr-immuno-oncology-research-fellow.why: AACR's Immuno-Oncology Research Fellowships are aimed directly at postdoctoral fellows working in immuno-oncology, a close match for Jordan's research on T-cell lymphoma, immunotherapy, and immune-based risk stratification. The two-year, $130,000 award would let him extend his single-cell and multi-omics immunology work while still in his current mentored postdoc role.

jhu-innovative-testing-research-earlier-.why: This program funds seed grants for novel early breast cancer detection methods, a disease area and clinical focus far from Jordan's T-cell lymphoma and blood cancer research. Both are technically oncology, but funding a breast-cancer-detection project would pull him away from the science he actually studies.

jhu-emerging-leader-award-the-mark-found.why: The Emerging Leader Award is a flagship early-career faculty grant meant for independent principal investigators, and Jordan is a senior postdoc without his own lab, so he isn't positioned to apply as lead PI yet. His cancer genomics and multi-omics expertise would make him a strong candidate here once he secures a faculty appointment.

cur-targeted-grants-in-mathematics-and-p.why: Simons' Targeted Grants in Mathematics and Physical Sciences fund high-risk theoretical math, physics, and computer science projects, a different discipline than the applied cancer genomics and single-cell immuno-oncology work Jordan does. Little in his current research program would naturally fit this program's scope, even given his computational and ML methods.
